dashboard.subtitle: Choose a module to start experimenting
dashboard.promptEngDesc: Experiment with generation parameters in real time across multiple LLM providers.
dashboard.fineTuningDesc: Learn how fine-tuning works — Full, LoRA, Prompt Tuning — with theory and a runnable code example with ROUGE evaluation.
dashboard.rlDesc: Train models with reinforcement learning from human feedback. Coming soon.

chat.newChatHint: Start a fresh conversation. Your current messages stay saved in the list; this clears the view so you can try a new topic or different parameters.
chat.noMessages: Start a conversation by typing a message below.

params.title: Parameters
params.apiKeyHint: Add API keys in .env (Groq, DeepSeek, OpenAI, Gemini) for better results and full parameter control.

methods.title: Parameter Guide
methods.techniquesIntro: Follow these rules to write clear, effective prompts (as in best-practice courses like ChatGPT Prompt Engineering for Developers):
methods.technique1Desc: State exactly what you want: task, format, length, and style. Vague prompts get vague answers.
methods.technique2Desc: Give the model role, audience, or domain (e.g. "You are a teacher explaining to a 10-year-old").
methods.technique3Desc: Use headings, bullet points, or steps. Delimit parts with ### or --- to separate instructions from content.
methods.technique4Desc: One or two input→output examples can lock in the format and behavior you want.
methods.technique5Desc: Refine the prompt based on the output. Add constraints or examples if the model drifts.
methods.tryIt: Try it — paste this into the chat:
methods.zeroShotDesc: Ask the model to do something with no examples. Best when the task is simple or the model is strong. For best classification results, use Groq or another API; the free HuggingFace small model may give inconsistent answers.
methods.zeroShotExample:
Classify the following sentence as positive, negative, or neutral:
"The food was great but the service was slow."
Sentiment:
methods.zeroShotParams: Temperature 0.3 · Max Tokens 30 · Top P 0.9 · Top K 40
methods.fewShotDesc: Include a few input–output examples in the prompt. The model infers the pattern and follows it.
methods.fewShotExample:
Classify the sentiment:

"I love this product!" → Positive
"Terrible experience." → Negative
"It was okay, nothing special." → Neutral
"The movie was absolutely fantastic!" →
methods.fewShotParams: Temperature 0.2 · Max Tokens 10 · Top P 0.85 · Top K 30
methods.chainOfThoughtDesc: Ask the model to "think step by step." It produces intermediate reasoning steps before the answer, improving accuracy.
methods.chainOfThoughtExample:
Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans of 3 tennis balls each. How many does he have now?

Let's think step by step.
methods.chainOfThoughtParams: Temperature 0.3 · Max Tokens 100 · Top P 0.9 · Top K 50
methods.temperatureDesc: Controls randomness. Lower values (0.1–0.3) make output focused and deterministic. Higher values (0.8–1.5) increase creativity and diversity. Very high values (>1.5) may produce incoherent text.
methods.maxTokensDesc: The maximum number of tokens the model will generate. One token is roughly 4 characters in English. Longer responses use more tokens and take longer to generate.
methods.topPDesc: Selects from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds P. A value of 0.9 means the model considers the top 90% probability mass. Lower values make output more focused.
methods.topKDesc: Limits generation to the K most likely next tokens. Lower values (10–20) make output more predictable. Higher values (50–100) allow more variety. Works together with Top P.

fineTuning.introDesc: Fine-tuning takes a pre-trained language model and trains it further on your own data so it becomes specialized for your task. Think of the pre-trained model as a student who has read every book in the library — fine-tuning is like giving them a focused course on the specific subject you need.
fineTuning.fullDesc: Updates every parameter in the model. Gives the best results but requires a lot of memory and compute (a powerful GPU). For large models (billions of parameters), this is expensive and slow. Best suited for when you have lots of data and a big GPU budget.
fineTuning.fullCons: Very high GPU cost · Risk of catastrophic forgetting
fineTuning.peftDesc: Instead of updating all parameters, LoRA freezes the original model and adds small trainable matrices to certain layers. Only these small matrices are trained — typically less than 1% of the total parameters. This makes fine-tuning fast, cheap, and possible even on consumer GPUs.
fineTuning.peftPros: Low GPU cost · Fast · Easy to share adapters
fineTuning.peftCons: Slightly lower quality than full fine-tuning
fineTuning.softDesc: Instead of changing model weights, you prepend a set of trainable "virtual tokens" (embeddings) to the input. The model itself stays frozen — only these few extra embeddings are trained. It is the lightest approach but works best on large models.
fineTuning.softPros: Lightest · Model stays frozen · Multiple tasks via different prompts
fineTuning.softCons: Works best on large models · Less flexible than LoRA
fineTuning.demoTitle: Live Demo: Base vs. Fine-tuned (with ROUGE)
fineTuning.demoDesc: This demo fine-tunes FLAN-T5-small with full fine-tuning (CPU-compatible), then compares base vs. fine-tuned outputs and ROUGE scores. LoRA and Prefix Tuning require full PyTorch with GPU and are shown in the code example for local use.
fineTuning.gpuUnavailable: GPU is not available. LoRA and Prefix Tuning require a GPU for efficient fine-tuning. Full fine-tuning was used instead (runs on CPU).
fineTuning.codeDesc: Below is the full code used in the demo. You can edit it, then copy and run locally (e.g. in a Jupyter notebook or Python script).
